title: Puppy Shifting Lameness: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Is your puppy limping on different legs? Learn about Panosteitis (growing pains), urgency levels, and how to help your dog today.
content_html:
What is Shifting Lameness in Puppies?

Shifting lameness occurs when a puppy appears to limp on one leg, only for the lameness to resolve and then reappear in a different leg days or weeks later. In growing puppies, this is most commonly associated with a condition called Panosteitis, often referred to as 'growing pains.' This involves inflammation on the surface of the long bones and typically affects large-breed dogs between 5 and 18 months of age.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for shifting lameness is Medium. While Panosteitis is generally not a life-threatening emergency, it is a very painful condition that requires veterinary intervention for pain management. You should schedule an appointment with your veterinarian within 24 to 48 hours. However, if your puppy is completely unable to stand, is lethargic, or has a high fever, you should seek immediate veterinary care.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Capturing a video of your puppy walking on a flat surface can be incredibly helpful for your veterinary team. Because lameness can be intermittent, your puppy might not show the limp once they reach the clinic due to adrenaline. A video allows the vet to see the exact gait abnormality, identify which legs are affected, and determine the severity of the discomfort before the physical exam begins.

faqs.question: Which dog breeds are most prone to shifting lameness?
faqs.answer: Large and giant breeds are most commonly affected, including German Shepherds, Golden Retrievers, Labrador Retrievers, and Doberman Pinschers.

faqs.question: How long does Panosteitis last?
faqs.answer: Episodes of lameness usually last 1 to 3 weeks per bone, but the entire condition can cycle through different legs until the puppy reaches skeletal maturity around 18-24 months.

faqs.question: What is the typical treatment for shifting lameness?
faqs.answer: Treatment usually involves rest, restricted exercise, and anti-inflammatory medications prescribed by a veterinarian to manage pain.
